title: The PMP Mindset
summary: How PMI expects a project manager to think on a PMBOK 8 exam: the mindset IS the principle-based, outcome-based way of working - servant leadership, value delivery, proactive and collaborative action, diagnose before acting, the least-drastic correct action, and tailoring to context. Each rule is framed around the 6 project-management principles.

sections.heading: The mindset is the principles
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle-based and outcome-based, not process-driven. The PMP mindset is simply behaving the way the 6 principles describe: serve people, deliver value, act proactively and collaboratively, diagnose before acting, take the least-drastic correct action, and tailor to context. When two answers both look valid, pick the one that delivers value and reflects a principle.

items.text: See the whole system; navigate complexity and optimise risk responses across interacting parts.
Treat value as the ultimate measure of success; adapt and enable change to deliver it.

items.code: Embed Quality Into Processes and Deliverables
items.text: Build quality into how the work is done and into what the project produces.
Lead with stewardship, integrity, and accountability regardless of role.

items.code: Integrate Sustainability Within All Project Areas
items.text: Weigh environmental, social, and economic sustainability throughout the project.
Foster a collaborative, accountable, and engaged team and stakeholder environment.

blocks.title: How to read a question
lines: Ask which principle the situation is really testing.
Prefer the option that advances an outcome, not just an activity.
Diagnose before treating; follow process before escalating.
Choose the least-drastic correct action every time.

sections.kicker: Be an Accountable Leader, Build an Empowered Culture
sections.heading: Lead with stewardship and service
sections.body: A PMP-minded project manager leads through service, stewardship, and respect for people. Stewardship means acting responsibly, caring for the team and resources, and focusing on value. On exam questions this usually means removing impediments, coaching, facilitating collaboration, and helping people succeed rather than controlling every detail. You protect the team environment and support psychological safety so issues surface early. If performance is weak, start by understanding the cause and respond with coaching, feedback, training, mentoring, or role clarification. The PMI Code of Ethics reinforces this through respect, responsibility, fairness, and honesty.

groups.label: Actions
lines: Remove impediments and enable the team.
Coach, mentor, and give feedback.
Facilitate collaboration and shared ownership.
Protect psychological safety so issues surface early.

groups.label: Weak answers
lines: Micromanage every detail.
Punish or threaten poor performance.
Abruptly remove someone without due process.
Blame instead of diagnosing the cause.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Favour choices that coach, support, and develop people over choices that punish, threaten, or abruptly remove them.

sections.heading: Assess first, then act
sections.body: The best first move is usually to assess the situation before taking action. Review the relevant plan, artifact, agreement, backlog, risk information, issue information, or performance data. Understand the cause, impact, and context before deciding what to do. This reflects navigating complexity and seeing the whole system rather than reacting to a symptom.

items.title: Gather the facts
lines: Review the plan, artifact, backlog, or performance data.

items.title: Understand cause and impact
lines: Predictive: check approved plans and baselines.
Agile / hybrid: inspect the backlog, working agreements, review and retrospective feedback.

items.title: Then decide
lines: Only after you understand the situation, choose the response.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: When several answers seem plausible, prefer investigate, review, analyse, or assess before implement, escalate, or reject.

sections.kicker: Adopt a Holistic View, Embed Quality, Focus on Value
sections.heading: Be proactive about risk, quality, and value
sections.body: A PMP-minded project manager does not wait for problems to become crises. You identify risks early, watch for warning signs, address impediments quickly, and use root-cause analysis to solve the underlying problem. Preventing a defect, conflict, or delay is usually stronger than reacting after the damage is done. PMBOK 8 emphasises building quality in, optimising risk responses, and protecting value throughout the project. Look for actions that prevent recurrence, improve the system, and protect value rather than temporary fixes that only treat symptoms.

groups.label: Actions
lines: Identify risks early and watch for warning signs.
Use root-cause analysis to fix the underlying problem.
Build quality in; prevent defects.
Protect value and improve the system.

groups.label: Weak answers
lines: Wait for a problem to become a crisis.
Apply a quick patch that treats a symptom.
Ignore warning signs.
Let the same defect recur.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Prefer prevention over firefighting, and root-cause analysis over a quick patch.

sections.heading: Tailor the approach to the work
sections.body: PMBOK 8 expects you to choose an approach that fits the project context. It is principle-based and explicitly supports tailoring - models, methods, and artifacts are chosen, not mandated. Do not force predictive, agile, or hybrid practices where they do not fit.

blocks.title: Match the approach to the context

rows.value: Use when scope is stable and control of planned work matters.

Use when frequent feedback, adaptability, and iterative delivery matter. Watch for incremental delivery, backlog refinement, reviews, retrospectives, servant leadership, and product-owner collaboration.
Use when part of the work needs predictability and another part benefits from adaptation.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Choose the answer that fits the project's context and delivery approach, not the one that applies a method mechanically.

sections.heading: Engage stakeholders early and communicate directly
sections.body: Stakeholder engagement is continuous, not a one-time activity. You identify stakeholders, analyse their needs and influence, engage them appropriately, and communicate in ways that support understanding and commitment. For conflict, confusion, or sensitive issues, direct conversation is usually better than a passive message trail. Speak with the people involved, clarify expectations, listen actively, and work toward resolution. Email can document decisions, but it is often not the best first tool for resolving disagreement or building alignment.

items.title: Identify and analyse
lines: Find stakeholders; assess needs, influence, and interest.

items.title: Engage continuously
lines: Engage appropriately throughout, not once at the start.

items.title: Communicate to resolve
lines: For conflict or sensitive issues, talk directly.
Clarify expectations and listen actively.

blocks.title: Cue
lines: Prefer direct communication and active engagement before escalation or one-way broadcast updates.
Email documents decisions; it rarely resolves disagreement.

sections.heading: Handle change through the appropriate system
sections.body: Do not implement uncontrolled change, and do not dismiss change without evaluation. The right response depends on the delivery approach.

groups.label: Response
lines: Assess impact.
Follow formal change control.
Obtain required approval.
Update plans, documents, and baselines.

groups.label: Response
lines: Change is expected.
Capture the request in the product backlog.
Product owner evaluates and reprioritises with team and stakeholder input.

groups.label: Response
lines: Determine which parts follow formal change control.
Manage the rest adaptively.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Predictive: evaluate impact and use change control. Agile: capture and reprioritise through the backlog. Never make a hidden or uncontrolled change.

sections.heading: Resolve at the lowest level; escalate when needed
sections.body: The project manager owns coordination and problem solving within their authority. That usually means trying reasonable actions first: clarify, analyse, facilitate, negotiate, replan, engage the right stakeholder, or work with the team to resolve the issue. Going straight to the sponsor or senior management is usually not the best first step unless the issue is truly beyond your authority or requires formal escalation. Extreme answers are often weak answers.

items.title: Act within your authority first
lines: Clarify, analyse, facilitate, negotiate, or replan.
Engage the right stakeholder or work with the team.

items.title: Escalate only when needed
lines: Escalate when the matter truly exceeds your authority or requires formal escalation.

blocks.title: Eliminate the extremes
lines: Ignore the issue.
Immediately escalate to the sponsor.
Remove someone without due process.
Force a decision without analysis.

sections.kicker: Be an Accountable Leader, Embed Quality
sections.heading: Act ethically and decide transparently
sections.body: Ethics is part of the PMP mindset, not a separate topic. The PMI Code of Ethics expects responsibility, respect, fairness, and honesty. On scenario questions this means telling the truth, disclosing issues appropriately, avoiding favoritism, protecting confidentiality, following agreed processes, and making decisions in the best interests of the project and organization. Ethical choices also build trust, which improves team performance and stakeholder engagement.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Tell the truth; disclose issues appropriately.
Treat people fairly; avoid favoritism.
Protect confidentiality.
Follow agreed processes and act in the project's best interest.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Hide information.
Manipulate stakeholders.
Bypass process for convenience.
Treat people unfairly.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: When in doubt, choose the answer that is transparent, fair, respectful, and responsible.

sections.heading: Think in outcomes, not just activities
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is about enabling outcomes, not only performing process steps correctly. It focuses on value delivery and performance domains rather than a purely process-driven mindset. When several options seem technically acceptable, the stronger choice usually improves outcomes: delivering value, supporting the team, engaging stakeholders, reducing risk, and aligning the project with organizational goals. This matters most in hybrid and agile questions, where adaptability and feedback count as much as plan compliance.

blocks.title: Outcome over activity
lines: Value delivered beats a procedurally correct but low-value step.
Supporting the team and stakeholders advances outcomes.
Reducing risk and aligning with goals is a stronger choice.
In agile and hybrid, feedback and adaptability weigh as much as plan compliance.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Choose the option that best advances value, collaboration, and sound outcomes, not just procedural activity.

source: Drawn from the PMP Examination Content Outline, PMBOK Guide (principles and performance domains), Agile Practice Guide, and the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct; reframed around the 6 project-management principles for PMBOK 8.
